name: The Long Betrayal
provenance: Told in the Underdark · Travels as scratched stone-records and whispers
summary: Three hundred years of trade ended when they sealed the door on our dead and called it self-defense.
content:
They call it a war. We call it the Sealing.

For centuries the trade routes were open. Our mushrooms for their grain. Our stonework for their timber. Our gems for their wool, their leather, their light. Three hundred years of commerce. Three hundred years of the surface pretending we were people when we had something they wanted to buy.

Then the Sun-Mad took the throne in the Silver City. He didn't see traders crossing his gates. He saw a disease that had learned to carry coin.

The war did not start with a blade. It started with a door.

They collapsed the Great North Gate while three hundred of our merchants were still inside the tunnel — halfway to the surface, halfway to a deal, halfway between two worlds that had agreed, briefly, to share the road. They sealed the door and walked away. They didn't kill them with steel. They killed them with air and time, and those are slower and quieter and leave no one to testify.

Then the Sentinel stood before the High Lords of the Silver City and said: *our merchants have been taken.* He called it a kidnapping. He used the silence of three hundred dead people — people whose names we still recite every year in the stone-halls, whose names he never learned — to justify the first descent.

We fought because we had no other door. Because they had sealed the last one on top of our dead and called it self-defense.

They didn't start this war to win. They started it to make certain there would be no one left to remember it differently.

We remember it differently.
